text:
HEMATOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — OUTPATIENT CLINIC NOTE
Response Assessment Visit — Cycle 4 FOLFOX/Avastin

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Medical Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology / Gastrointestinal Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient — Treatment Response Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Marcus T. Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic/Latino male with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutated, MSS) with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases who presents today for cycle 4 response assessment following CT restaging showing partial response to FOLFOX plus Avastin.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Delgado is well-known to our gastrointestinal oncology service. He was initially diagnosed with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 8-9 weeks ago following presentation with a change in bowel habits, left-sided abdominal cramping, and an elevated CEA of 487 ng/mL. Colonoscopy demonstrated a 4.5 cm sigmoid mass, biopsy-confirmed as moderately differentiated (grade 2) adenocarcinoma, CDX2+, CK20+, CK7-, SATB2+, MMR-proficient (MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2 all retained by IHC). Staging CT and MRI liver confirmed four bilobar hepatic metastases (largest 3.8 cm in segment VI), with no evidence of extrahepatic disease beyond the primary and liver at that time.

Comprehensive molecular profiling via NGS (523-gene panel) from the sigmoid primary revealed KRAS p.G12D mutation (VAF 42%, confirmed pathogenic driver), with co-occurring alterations in APC p.R1450* (VAF 48%), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 45%), SMAD4 p.R361H (VAF 31%), PIK3CA p.E545K (VAF 19%), and FBXW7 p.R465H (VAF 14%). MYC amplification and 18q loss also identified. The KRAS G12D mutation confirmed ineligibility for anti-EGFR therapy (Erbitux or pani), and MSS/low-TMB status (3.2 mut/Mb) confirmed ineligibility for pembro or other checkpoint inhibitors in this frontline setting.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion — with consensus from medical oncology, hepatobiliary surgery, colorectal surgery, and interventional radiology — the patient was deemed unresectable at presentation given bilobar hepatic distribution and the need to preserve adequate future liver remnant. A conversion chemotherapy strategy was elected, with the primary sigmoid tumor assessed as non-obstructing and not requiring urgent surgical intervention. Treatment was initiated with FOLFOX (Eloxatin 85 mg/m2 D1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 D1, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus D1, then 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 CI over 46 hours) plus bev 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks.

The patient has now completed 4 cycles of FOLFOX/Avastin. Restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed after completion of cycle 4 (approximately 8 weeks from treatment initiation) and demonstrated encouraging tumor reduction: the segment VI hepatic lesion decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.9 cm, segment IVa from 2.1 to 1.6 cm, segment II from 1.3 to 1.0 cm, and segment VII from 0.9 to 0.6 cm. The primary sigmoid mass appears slightly smaller at approximately 3.9 cm. No new lesions were identified, and no pulmonary metastases were seen. CEA has trended down from a baseline of 487 ng/mL to 198 ng/mL — a 59% reduction — which is gratifying and consistent with the radiographic response.

This was reviewed informally with our hepatobiliary surgical colleague ahead of today's clinic visit. Despite the objective tumor shrinkage, the bilobar distribution of hepatic disease remains unchanged (lesions persist in segments II, IVa, VI, and VII), and the estimated future liver remnant following any hypothetical resection of all disease-bearing segments would be insufficient — estimated at less than 25-30% of total liver volume — making conversion hepatectomy not yet feasible. The consensus is to continue toward the planned 12-cycle course and reassess at that endpoint.

The patient presents today for formal review of imaging findings, discussion of toxicity management, and determination of the treatment plan going forward.

From a symptom standpoint, Mr. Delgado reports the following:

- NEUROPATHY: He has been experiencing tingling and numbness in his fingertips and toes since approximately cycle 3, initially described as intermittent and cold-triggered. At today's visit, he notes the neuropathy has become somewhat more persistent, particularly in the fingertips, where he experiences near-constant tingling. The classic cold-induced dysesthesias remain prominent — he describes experiencing sharp, shooting sensations when handling cold objects and has been consistently using gloves since we counseled him on cold avoidance. He is also noticing early, emerging sensory change in the dorsum of his feet, though this is less prominent than the hand symptoms. No significant functional impairment reported at this time — he can still button his shirt and write on the board at school, though he notes some slowing. He rates the neuropathy as 4/10 in bothersome-ness and 2/10 for pain.

- HYPERTENSION: He was placed on amlodipine 5 mg daily at approximately cycle 3 for new Avastin-related hypertension (grade 1, BP 154/96 mmHg). Today's blood pressure is 142/88 mmHg, which represents meaningful improvement though is still mildly above goal. He denies headaches, visual changes, chest pain, or shortness of breath. He has been coordinating with his primary care physician regarding antihypertensive management and reports medication compliance.

- GI SYMPTOMS: He reports mild intermittent nausea on the day of infusion and the following morning, well-managed with the prescribed prochlorperazine. Appetite remains intact. No significant mucositis. No constipation or diarrhea beyond what is expected. Bowel habits are generally every 1-2 days, semi-formed stool, no blood noted.

- FATIGUE: He describes mild to moderate fatigue, particularly during the week following infusion (days 3-7), with recovery toward his baseline by the second week. He has returned to part-time teaching duties on a modified schedule, which he finds meaningful and wishes to continue as long as possible.

- PORT/INFUSION SITE: No issues with the implanted port. No signs of infection or thrombosis.

He denies chest pain, dyspnea, hematochezia, melena, significant abdominal pain, skin rash, or urinary symptoms. He has not experienced any grade 3-4 toxicities to date requiring hospitalization or emergency evaluation.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

- Constitutional: Mild fatigue (as above). No fever, chills, or sweats. Weight stable (patient reports no appreciable weight change, confirmed approximately flat on clinic scale).
- HEENT: No headaches, no visual disturbances.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis.
- Cardiovascular: Mild hypertension as noted; no chest pain, palpitations, edema.
- Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea controlled with antiemetics. No significant mucositis, diarrhea, constipation, rectal bleeding, or melena. No abdominal pain beyond mild baseline discomfort.
- Neurological: Peripheral sensory neuropathy grade 1-2 as described — fingertips bilaterally (predominant), toes and dorsal feet (emerging); cold-induced dysesthesias ongoing. No gait instability, no weakness, no fall.
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain or new joint complaints.
- Dermatologic: No hand-foot syndrome, no rash.
- Genitourinary: No hematuria, no dysuria. Proteinuria not formally checked since last visit — will obtain today's urine spot protein/creatinine ratio given Avastin use.
- Hematologic: No unusual bleeding or bruising.
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Diabetes well managed on metformin, home blood glucose monitoring readings acceptable per patient.
- Psychiatric: Mild anxiety about diagnosis and prognosis, which he describes as manageable. Good family and social support.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS) — as above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well controlled on metformin, HbA1c 6.8% at diagnosis
3. Hypertension — new/worsening in context of Avastin; now on amlodipine
4. Eloxatin-induced peripheral neuropathy (emerging, grade 1-2)
5. Former smoker (quit approximately 12 years prior, approximately 15-20 pack-year history)

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Mr. Delgado is a high school teacher (science), lives with his wife, and has two adult children. He is a former smoker who quit approximately 12 years ago. He drinks alcohol rarely (1-2 drinks per month). He is physically active at baseline; currently on modified work schedule. Excellent family support system; wife accompanies him to clinic visits. He is Spanish-speaking as his primary language; English fluency good. Primary support: wife and adult children. Lives in a single-story home, no stairs of concern.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Father: colorectal cancer (deceased, age 71). Mother: hypertension, type 2 diabetes. No family history of BRCA-related cancers or Lynch syndrome-associated tumors reported. No other known first-degree relatives with colorectal or GI malignancies.

Germline testing from NGS panel: no pathogenic variants in APC, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, or MUTYH — no hereditary colorectal cancer syndrome identified.

ALLERGIES:
NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies)

MEDICATIONS (Current):
1. FOLFOX (oxali 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 bolus + 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 CI over 46 hours) every 2 weeks — cycle 4 completed
2. bev (Avastin) 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks
3. Metformin 1000 mg PO BID (for type 2 DM)
4. Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (for bev-related hypertension)
5. Ondansetron 8 mg PO Q8H PRN nausea (infusion day and following 24-48 hours)
6. Prochlorperazine 10 mg PO Q6H PRN breakthrough nausea
7. Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO PRN anticipatory nausea/anxiety
8. Calcium and magnesium supplementation IV with each infusion (per institutional Eloxatin protocol)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 36.9°C (oral)
- Heart Rate: 78 bpm, regular
- Blood Pressure: 142/88 mmHg (left arm, seated, after 5 minutes rest)
- Respiratory Rate: 16 breaths/min
- SpO2: 98% on room air
- Weight: 81.2 kg (stable from prior visits)
- Height: 174 cm
- BSA: 1.97 m2
- ECOG Performance Status: 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out work of a light or sedentary nature)

General: Alert and oriented x3, well-appearing, in no acute distress. Conversational, pleasant. Accompanied by his wife.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx moist, no mucositis, no oral lesions. No icterus.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Neck supple.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 present. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in the left lower quadrant overlying the sigmoid primary tumor region — unchanged from prior visits, not peritoneal in character. No guarding, no rebound. Liver edge palpable at approximately 1-2 cm below the right costal margin — mildly enlarged, non-tender. Spleen not palpable. No ascites appreciated. Bowel sounds present and normoactive. Implanted port site (right anterior chest wall, subclavicular): clean, non-erythematous, non-tender, no signs of infection.

Extremities: No clubbing, no cyanosis. Trace bilateral ankle edema (1+), likely related to bev and/or calcium channel blocker effect of amlodipine — stable and mild.

Neurological:
- Cranial nerves II-XII intact.
- Reduced light touch and pinprick sensation in fingertips bilaterally (more pronounced on index fingers and thumbs) and in toes bilaterally, extending minimally onto the dorsal foot. Sensation in the palms and dorsum of hands preserved. Vibration sense at great toes mildly diminished bilaterally.
- Deep tendon reflexes: 1+ patellar and Achilles bilaterally (mildly reduced but present); 2+ brachioradialis and biceps bilaterally.
- Gait: normal stride, tandem gait intact without difficulty, no ataxia.
- Romberg test: negative.
- Grip strength: 4+/5 bilaterally, symmetric; hand coordination grossly intact.
- This neuropathy pattern is consistent with grade 1-2 Eloxatin-induced sensory neuropathy — sensory predominant, distal-predominant, length-dependent pattern.

Skin: No palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome). No rash. No ecchymoses. Skin intact. No concerning nail changes.

Lymph nodes: No palpable adenopathy in cervical, axillary, or inguinal regions.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today or most recent):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mild anemia, unchanged from prior)
- Hematocrit: 35.2%
- MCV: 88 fL
- Platelets: 218 x10^9/L
- Neutrophils: 3.4 x10^9/L (ANC adequate for treatment)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Chloride: 101 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 25 mEq/L
- BUN: 18 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 0.94 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
- ALT: 38 U/L (upper limit of normal 40)
- AST: 42 U/L (mildly elevated, stable — consistent with hepatic metastatic burden)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 118 U/L (mildly elevated, stable)
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL
- Glucose: 122 mg/dL (non-fasting)

Tumor Markers:
- CEA: 198 ng/mL (down from 487 ng/mL at baseline; prior value 4 weeks ago was approximately 290 ng/mL — continued downward trend)
- CA 19-9: not formally obtained at this visit; last value 312 U/mL at diagnosis

Coagulation: INR 1.1 (normal)

Urine spot protein/creatinine ratio (obtained today): 0.18 (within acceptable range, <0.5; no clinically significant proteinuria — Avastin can be continued)

HbA1c (last checked approximately 4 weeks ago): 6.9% — stable, acceptable glycemic control

---

IMAGING RESULTS (CT CAP with contrast, most recent restaging scan after cycle 4):

The restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrates an objective tumor reduction across all measured target lesions:
- Hepatic segment VI lesion: 2.9 cm (down from 3.8 cm at baseline)
- Hepatic segment IVa lesion: 1.6 cm (down from 2.1 cm)
- Hepatic segment II lesion: 1.0 cm (down from 1.3 cm)
- Hepatic segment VII lesion: 0.6 cm (down from 0.9 cm)
- Primary sigmoid mass: 3.9 cm (slightly reduced from 4.5 cm)
- No new hepatic lesions. No pulmonary nodules. No peritoneal disease.
- Sum of measured target lesion diameters decreased from approximately 8.1 cm to 6.1 cm — a 25% reduction, satisfying criteria for partial response.

This result was reviewed with our hepatobiliary surgical colleagues. Despite encouraging downsizing, the bilobar pattern of hepatic involvement persists — lesions remain in both the left lobe (segments II and IVa) and right lobe (segments VI and VII). Surgical planning was informally discussed; the team concurs that the future liver remnant following complete hepatic clearance would be insufficient at this juncture (estimated FLR <25%). The surgical team has indicated they would be willing to reassess after the completion of the full 12-cycle induction course, at which time we may also explore portal vein embolization if needed to hypertrophy the FLR, or a two-stage hepatectomy approach if appropriate. Additionally, ablative options (radiofrequency ablation or microwave ablation) for smaller lesions may be discussed in a future tumor board.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. STAGE IV SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA (KRAS G12D, MSS), WITH BILOBAR HEPATIC METASTASES — PARTIAL RESPONSE TO FOLFOX/bev:

Mr. Delgado is showing a meaningful, though not yet sufficient-for-resection, partial response to first-line FOLFOX plus bev after 4 cycles. The CEA trajectory is encouraging, having dropped from 487 to 198 ng/mL — a 59% reduction — which mirrors the radiographic findings. The goal of the conversion chemotherapy strategy remains operative: to reduce hepatic tumor burden to a point where complete oncologic resection with adequate FLR is feasible.

Given the continued bilobar involvement and insufficient FLR, the plan is to continue with the full intended 12-cycle course of FOLFOX/Avastin. Formal re-presentation to the hepatobiliary multidisciplinary team will occur after cycle 8 for interim reassessment and again after cycle 12 for definitive surgical planning. We will maintain FOLFOX at current doses (Eloxatin 85 mg/m2) unless dose-limiting toxicity mandates reduction.

Molecular landscape: KRAS G12D mutation — anti-EGFR therapies remain contraindicated. BRAF wild-type. MSS. PIK3CA p.E545K co-mutation and SMAD4 loss are noted but do not change current management (anti-EGFR is already precluded by KRAS mutation). No clinical trials with KRAS G12D-directed agents are currently available for this patient; this will continue to be monitored.

2. Eloxatin-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY, GRADE 1-2:

Mr. Delgado has developed grade 1-2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, with predominantly cold dysesthesias and emerging persistent numbness in the fingertips and early sensory changes in the feet. This is an expected, cumulative, and unfortunately dose-limiting toxicity of oxali. At this point, the neuropathy is grade 1-2 and is not yet meeting criteria for dose modification (our institutional threshold for Eloxatin dose reduction is grade 2 with functional impairment or grade 3).

Management plan:
- Continue cold avoidance counseling: cold beverages, cold air exposure, cold tap water — gloves, warm socks, avoidance of cold foods/drinks during infusion days and the following 5-7 days.
- Maintain intravenous calcium and magnesium infusions with each cycle (though evidence for neuropathy prevention is limited, this is our institutional standard practice and is benign).
- Patient to notify our team immediately if neuropathy progresses to grade 2 with functional impairment (difficulty with ADLs) or grade 3 at any time — at which point oxali dose reduction to 75 mg/m2 or potential discontinuation will be implemented.
- Baseline neurological exam documented today; will reassess at each visit.
- No pharmacologic neuropathy prophylaxis agents (duloxetine has modest neuropathy treatment data; vitamin B6, vitamin E, and glutamine not recommended based on current evidence).
- Plan to reassess at next visit (cycle 6 or post-cycle 6 check).

3. bev-RELATED HYPERTENSION, GRADE 1 (IMPROVING):

Blood pressure today is 142/88 mmHg on amlodipine 5 mg daily, which is improved from the peak of 154/96 mmHg at cycle 3 when antihypertensive therapy was initiated. This remains mildly above target (goal BP <140/90 mmHg in cancer patients; ideally <130/80 mmHg). Grade 1 bev-related hypertension managed appropriately with calcium channel blockade. No proteinuria, no thromboembolic symptoms to suggest other bev toxicities.

Plan:
- Continue amlodipine 5 mg daily. If BP at next visit remains above 140/90, will coordinate with PCP to uptitrate amlodipine to 10 mg or add an ACE inhibitor/ARB.
- Urine spot protein/creatinine ratio obtained today: 0.18 — no significant proteinuria, Avastin safe to continue.
- Avastin to continue at 5 mg/kg. Threshold for holding: diastolic >110 mmHg or any grade 3 hypertension uncontrolled on oral agents.
- Patient reminded of symptoms warranting emergency evaluation: severe headache, visual changes, hypertensive urgency/emergency symptoms.

4. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:
Continue metformin 1000 mg BID. HbA1c 6.9% — acceptable. Monitoring ongoing. No adjustments required. Patient reminded that dexamethasone premedication can transiently elevate blood glucose on infusion days — home monitoring encouraged and patient is aware.

5. CANCER-RELATED FATIGUE:
Mild to moderate, expected in the context of active chemotherapy with FOLFOX. Pattern consistent with expected nadir (days 3-7 post-infusion). ECOG PS 1 maintained. Patient has returned to part-time teaching, which is a positive functional indicator. No specific pharmacologic intervention at this time. Encouraged adequate rest, light activity as tolerated, nutrition. Patient declines formal referral to integrative oncology/exercise rehabilitation at this time but will reconsider.

6. PLAN SUMMARY:
- Proceed with cycle 5 of FOLFOX/Avastin at full doses (oxali 85 mg/m2, assuming neuropathy does not worsen prior to next treatment).
- Schedule cycle 6 and cycle 8 visits. Formal reassessment CT CAP after cycle 8 (at approximately 16 weeks from treatment start) for interim response evaluation and tumor board re-presentation.
- Repeat CEA and CBC/CMP with each treatment cycle.
- Consider CA 19-9 re-check at cycle 6 visit.
- Refer back to hepatobiliary surgical team after cycle 8 imaging for formal resectability re-assessment.
- Continue blood pressure monitoring at each cycle; coordinate with PCP on antihypertensive adjustments as needed.
- Patient education reinforced regarding neuropathy progression reporting, cold avoidance, and hypertension symptoms.
- Return to clinic in 2 weeks for cycle 5 of FOLFOX/Avastin and CBC/CMP check.

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes. More than 50% of visit time spent counseling on treatment response, neuropathy management, and goals of therapy.

Patient and wife understand the plan. Questions addressed. Patient remains motivated and engaged. ECOG PS 1.

[Electronically signed by Attending Medical Oncologist, Gastrointestinal Oncology]
